task.title: Using iproute2 to temporarily configure and enable multiple paths for MPTCP applications

context.p: Each MPTCP connection uses a single subflow similar to plain TCP. To get the MPTCP benefits, specify a higher limit for maximum number of subflows for each MPTCP connection. Then configure additional endpoints to create those subflows.

note.p: The configuration in this procedure will not persist after rebooting your machine.

context.p: Note that MPTCP does not yet support mixed IPv6 and IPv4 endpoints for the same socket. Use endpoints belonging to the same address family.

step.cmd: Configure the client to accept up to 1 additional remote address, as provided by the server:
Add IP address 198.51.100.1 as a new MPTCP endpoint on the server:

info.codeblock: # ip mptcp endpoint add 198.51.100.1 dev enp1s0 signal
info.p: The signal option ensures that the ADD_ADDR packet is sent after the three-way-handshake.

step.cmd: Start the iperf3 server, and force it to create MPTCP sockets instead of TCP sockets:

info.codeblock:
# mptcpize run iperf3 -s
Server listening on 5201

step.cmd: Connect the client to the server, and force it to create MPTCP sockets instead of TCP sockets:

li.p: Verify the connection and IP address limit:
Verify MPTCP counters by using the nstat MPTcp* command on a server:
li.codeblock:
# nstat MPTcp*
#kernel MPTcpExtMPCapableSYNRX 2 0.0 MPTcpExtMPCapableACKRX 2 0.0 MPTcpExtMPJoinSynRx 2 0.0 MPTcpExtMPJoinAckRx 2 0.0 MPTcpExtEchoAdd 2 0.0
